# Transcrição: 6. Assuming Your Lead Has Not Done Their Homework

- **Fonte:** 6. Assuming Your Lead Has Not Done Their Homework
- **Duração:** 02:42
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 02:42]

Assuming your new lead has not done their homework is a mistake. Look, the internet makes it easy to comparison shop. For you to think that they have it is wrong. The good news is by the time the shopper submits a lead to you, they're most likely ready to have a serious conversation about purchasing. That's why speed matters because otherwise you're going to lose their attention. The bad news here is this. They have information. They may have read product reviews about your product, reviews about your company. Maybe they've seen something about competition or possibly confused about either products, offering prices. Hey, it's all out there. But that's the beauty of the internet. The leads from the website. People want choices and you get a shot at more prospective buyers, but you're going to have to work through it. You're going to have to find out what they know, what they don't know. When you receive a lead from an internet buyer, you don't want to treat that buyer like a cold call or someone that has just walked off the street. You have to know they know something about the watch they looked at, the refrigerator, the insurance policy, whatever it is they're searching, maybe the real estate, the house, the mortgage, it goes on and on. A well-informed buyer is a great sales prospect, but you, by missing the fact that they are educated, can now miss or mistreat them. Focus on correcting any misconceptions, but to do that, you have to know what they know, what they think, what they don't know, what they've looked at and not looked at before you start trying to convince them of your product, your company. So take a moment to ask people, have you looked at other sites? Have you collected other information? Most of our internet buyers, most of our opportunities here have done tremendous amounts of research, and I don't want to waste your time giving you information that either you're not interested in or you already know about. The business buyer, B2B, researching products online has also gone through the process of researching, making comparisons, possibly already deciding what their budget is. When you first contact that buyer, consumer or business to business, make sure you've done some studying up on that lead prior to responding. How do you do that? God, it's so easy. All you've got to do is do what they did to find you. Search out their names, search out their company, look at maybe other people that are doing business with them. It's all over the net. If I ever hit your website, if I ever mystery shop you, look, I'm going to leave clues. My name's going to be there, the company's going to be there, and all you have to do is Google Grant Cardone, and you'll know everything about me. You'll know how to treat me. You'll know how to follow me up, and you'll have a lead up on this lead in your marketplace.
